benchmark: The student will participate in and contribute to conversations, group discussions, and oral presentations.
item_specs: Items assess students' ability to plan and deliver oral presentations with supporting evidence.
essential_questions: How do effective speakers engage their audience?
Why is active listening important in discussions?
learning_targets: I can participate in group discussions using evidence to support my ideas.
I can deliver an organized oral presentation.
sample_assessment: Which is the BEST way to support a claim in a group discussion? A) Repeating the claim louder B) Providing evidence from the text C) Changing the subject D) Agreeing with everyone

benchmark: The student will use word analysis strategies and word reference materials to determine the meanings of unfamiliar words and phrases.
item_specs: Items require using context clues, prefixes, suffixes, and roots to determine word meaning.
essential_questions: How can word parts help us figure out unknown words?
learning_targets: I can use context clues to determine word meanings.
I can use prefixes, suffixes, and roots to analyze word meanings.
sample_assessment: In the sentence 'The benevolent king shared his wealth with the poor,' what does 'benevolent' most likely mean? A) Cruel B) Kind C) Wealthy D) Powerful

benchmark: The student will read and demonstrate comprehension of fictional texts, narrative nonfiction, and poetry, analyzing how authors use literary devices.
item_specs: Items require identifying theme, analyzing character development, and recognizing literary devices such as simile, metaphor, and personification.
essential_questions: How do authors use literary devices to create meaning?
How does analyzing a text deepen understanding?
learning_targets: I can identify and explain the theme of a text.
I can analyze how an author uses literary devices to develop meaning.
sample_assessment: Which sentence contains a metaphor? A) The wind howled like a wolf. B) She is a shining star in our class. C) The leaves danced gently. D) He ran as fast as a cheetah.

benchmark: The student will read and demonstrate comprehension of nonfiction texts, identifying main idea, supporting details, and organizational patterns.
item_specs: Items require identifying central ideas, distinguishing between facts and opinions, and recognizing text structures.
essential_questions: How do we determine the main idea of a nonfiction text?
How does text structure support comprehension?
learning_targets: I can identify the main idea and supporting details in nonfiction.
I can recognize organizational patterns such as cause/effect and compare/contrast.
sample_assessment: Which organizational pattern does an article use when it describes the reasons for and results of the American Revolution? A) Compare and contrast B) Cause and effect C) Problem and solution D) Chronological order

benchmark: The student will write in a variety of forms, with a focus on narrative, expository, and persuasive writing, using the writing process.
item_specs: Items assess planning, drafting, revising, and editing of multi-paragraph compositions.
essential_questions: How does the writing process improve the quality of writing?
How do different purposes change the way we write?
learning_targets: I can write a multi-paragraph essay with a clear thesis and supporting details.
I can use the writing process to plan, draft, revise, and edit my writing.
sample_assessment: Which is the BEST thesis statement for a persuasive essay about school lunches? A) School lunches are served daily. B) Many students eat lunch at school. C) Schools should offer healthier lunch options to improve student performance. D) Lunch is my favorite part of the day.

benchmark: The student will use correct grammar, usage, capitalization, punctuation, and spelling when writing.
item_specs: Items assess correct use of subject-verb agreement, pronoun-antecedent agreement, punctuation, and capitalization.
essential_questions: Why is correct grammar and mechanics important in writing?
learning_targets: I can use correct subject-verb agreement.
I can use commas, semicolons, and end punctuation correctly.
sample_assessment: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement? A) The dogs runs fast. B) The dogs run fast. C) The dogs is fast. D) The dogs be fast.

benchmark: The student will find, evaluate, and select appropriate resources for a research product and cite sources correctly.
item_specs: Items assess ability to identify credible sources, avoid plagiarism, and use proper citation format.
essential_questions: How do we determine if a source is credible?
Why is it important to cite sources?
learning_targets: I can evaluate sources for credibility and relevance.
I can cite sources correctly to avoid plagiarism.
sample_assessment: Which source would be MOST credible for a research report on climate change? A) A personal blog B) A peer-reviewed scientific journal C) A social media post D) An advertisement

benchmark: The student will give and seek information in conversations, in group discussions, and in oral presentations, citing evidence to support opinions.
item_specs: Items assess ability to present claims supported by evidence in formal and informal speaking situations.
essential_questions: How does evidence strengthen oral communication?
learning_targets: I can present a claim supported by relevant evidence.
I can respond to others' ideas by building on or respectfully challenging them.
sample_assessment: When responding to a classmate's argument in a discussion, which approach is MOST effective? A) Ignoring their point B) Acknowledging their point and providing a counterclaim with evidence C) Raising your voice D) Changing the topic

benchmark: The student will develop vocabulary by using context, structure, and connotations of words, including figurative language.
item_specs: Items require interpreting figurative language and distinguishing between connotation and denotation.
essential_questions: How does word choice affect meaning and tone?
learning_targets: I can distinguish between connotation and denotation.
I can interpret figurative language including idioms, analogies, and allusions.
sample_assessment: Which word has the most POSITIVE connotation? A) Cheap B) Economical C) Stingy D) Miserly

benchmark: The student will read and demonstrate comprehension of fictional texts, narrative nonfiction, and poetry, analyzing how authors develop theme and character.
item_specs: Items require analyzing how characters change, how themes develop, and how narrative techniques create effects.
essential_questions: How do authors develop complex characters?
How does conflict drive theme development?
learning_targets: I can analyze how a character changes throughout a story.
I can explain how the author develops the theme through character, plot, and setting.
sample_assessment: A character who changes significantly from the beginning to the end of a story is called a — A) Static character B) Dynamic character C) Flat character D) Minor character

benchmark: The student will read and demonstrate comprehension of nonfiction texts, analyzing the author's purpose, point of view, and use of evidence.
item_specs: Items assess ability to determine author's purpose, distinguish between perspectives, and evaluate the strength of evidence.
essential_questions: How does an author's purpose shape a text?
How do we evaluate the evidence an author uses?
learning_targets: I can determine an author's purpose and point of view.
I can evaluate whether an author's evidence effectively supports the argument.
sample_assessment: An author writes, 'Every student deserves access to technology.' The author's purpose is most likely to — A) Entertain B) Persuade C) Describe D) Narrate

benchmark: The student will write in a variety of forms, with emphasis on exposition and persuasion, developing a clear thesis supported by evidence.
item_specs: Items assess the ability to write organized essays with a clear thesis, evidence, and logical reasoning.
essential_questions: How do we build a convincing argument in writing?
learning_targets: I can write a persuasive essay with a clear thesis and supporting evidence.
I can organize my writing with effective transitions.
sample_assessment: Which sentence would BEST serve as a transition between two body paragraphs? A) In conclusion B) Furthermore, this evidence supports C) Once upon a time D) I think that

benchmark: The student will edit writing for correct grammar, usage, capitalization, punctuation, and sentence structure.
item_specs: Items assess recognition of errors in grammar, punctuation, and sentence structure, including fragments and run-ons.
essential_questions: How does editing improve the clarity of writing?
learning_targets: I can identify and correct sentence fragments and run-on sentences.
I can use varied sentence structure in my writing.
sample_assessment: Which is a sentence fragment? A) The dog barked loudly. B) Running down the street. C) She went to the store and bought milk. D) After the game, we celebrated.

benchmark: The student will conduct research to answer questions, using multiple sources and evaluating credibility and accuracy.
item_specs: Items assess ability to synthesize information from multiple sources and evaluate source reliability.
essential_questions: How do we synthesize information from multiple sources?
learning_targets: I can gather information from multiple credible sources.
I can synthesize information to answer a research question.
sample_assessment: When researching a topic, which strategy is MOST effective? A) Using only one source B) Copying text directly without citation C) Using multiple credible sources and cross-referencing information D) Only using websites ending in .com

benchmark: The student will use interviewing techniques to gain information and present findings to an audience.
item_specs: Items assess the ability to formulate open-ended questions and organize findings for presentation.
essential_questions: How can interviews enhance research and understanding?
learning_targets: I can develop open-ended interview questions.
I can present interview findings in an organized manner.
sample_assessment: Which is the BEST open-ended interview question? A) Do you like your job? B) How has your career path shaped your perspective on education? C) What is your name? D) Is this your first job?

benchmark: The student will develop and expand vocabulary using context, word origins, roots, affixes, and analogies.
item_specs: Items assess knowledge of Greek and Latin roots and affixes, and the ability to complete analogies.
essential_questions: How do word origins help us understand vocabulary across content areas?
learning_targets: I can use Greek and Latin roots and affixes to determine word meaning.
I can complete and create analogies.
sample_assessment: The Latin root 'bene' means 'good.' Which word most likely means 'a good deed'? A) Benefit B) Beneath C) Bend D) Bench

benchmark: The student will read and analyze fiction and poetry, including how authors use elements of narrative structure and poetic forms to convey meaning.
item_specs: Items require analyzing narrative point of view, plot structure, figurative language in poetry, and the effect of form on meaning.
essential_questions: How does narrative structure shape meaning?
How does a poet's choices about form and language create meaning?
learning_targets: I can analyze how narrative point of view influences a story.
I can explain how a poem's form and figurative language contribute to its meaning.
sample_assessment: A poem written in three four-line stanzas with an ABAB rhyme scheme is most likely a — A) Haiku B) Free verse poem C) Ballad D) Limerick

benchmark: The student will read and analyze nonfiction texts, evaluating arguments, claims, evidence, and reasoning.
item_specs: Items require evaluating the strength of arguments, identifying logical fallacies, and analyzing rhetorical strategies.
essential_questions: How do we evaluate the strength of an argument?
What makes evidence relevant and sufficient?
learning_targets: I can evaluate the relevance and sufficiency of evidence in an argument.
I can identify logical fallacies in persuasive texts.
sample_assessment: Which rhetorical appeal does the following statement use: 'As a doctor with 20 years of experience, I can assure you this treatment is safe'? A) Ethos B) Pathos C) Logos D) Kairos

benchmark: The student will write in a variety of forms, including narrative, expository, and persuasive, with emphasis on developing a cohesive argument.
item_specs: Items assess the ability to construct well-organized arguments with evidence, counterarguments, and logical reasoning.
essential_questions: How do effective writers build and support a cohesive argument?
learning_targets: I can write an argumentative essay with a clear claim, evidence, and counterargument.
I can vary sentence structure for effect.
sample_assessment: In an argumentative essay, the purpose of a counterargument is to — A) Weaken your own position B) Show you have considered opposing views and can refute them C) Agree with the other side D) Change your thesis

benchmark: The student will edit for correct grammar, usage, mechanics, and sentence formation, including parallel structure and active voice.
item_specs: Items assess recognition of parallel structure errors, passive voice, misplaced modifiers, and other grammatical issues.
essential_questions: How do parallel structure and active voice improve writing clarity?
learning_targets: I can identify and correct errors in parallel structure.
I can revise passive voice sentences to active voice.
sample_assessment: Which sentence uses correct parallel structure? A) She likes swimming, biking, and to run. B) She likes swimming, biking, and running. C) She likes to swim, biking, and running. D) She likes swim, bike, and run.

benchmark: The student will make planned oral presentations, including analyzing and evaluating persuasive techniques in media messages.
item_specs: Items assess ability to deliver formal presentations and analyze persuasive techniques in various media.
essential_questions: How do media messages use persuasive techniques to influence audiences?
learning_targets: I can deliver a formal oral presentation with a clear message.
I can analyze persuasive techniques in media.
sample_assessment: An advertisement features a famous athlete endorsing a sports drink. This is an example of — A) Bandwagon B) Testimonial C) Plain folks D) Transfer

benchmark: The student will apply knowledge of word origins, derivations, and figurative language to extend vocabulary development in authentic texts.
item_specs: Items require applying knowledge of etymology and figurative language to determine meaning in context.
essential_questions: How does understanding word origins enhance reading comprehension?
learning_targets: I can use word origins and derivations to determine meaning.
I can interpret figurative language in complex texts.
sample_assessment: The phrase 'jumbo shrimp' is an example of — A) Simile B) Metaphor C) Oxymoron D) Hyperbole

benchmark: The student will read and analyze a variety of literary texts, including how authors use literary elements to develop theme across different genres.
item_specs: Items require analyzing how literary elements such as symbolism, irony, and allegory develop theme across genres.
essential_questions: How do literary elements work together to develop a theme?
learning_targets: I can analyze how authors use symbolism, irony, and allegory.
I can compare theme development across different genres.
sample_assessment: In a story, the audience knows the villain is hiding behind the door, but the character does not. This is an example of — A) Verbal irony B) Situational irony C) Dramatic irony D) Sarcasm

benchmark: The student will read, interpret, analyze, and evaluate nonfiction texts, including informational and persuasive texts from various sources.
item_specs: Items assess ability to analyze structure, evaluate arguments, and compare perspectives across nonfiction texts.
essential_questions: How do we critically evaluate nonfiction texts from different perspectives?
learning_targets: I can analyze the structure and purpose of nonfiction texts.
I can evaluate arguments and compare perspectives across sources.
sample_assessment: Which text feature is MOST useful for determining if a news article may be biased? A) The headline B) The photographs C) The author's background and publication source D) The word count

benchmark: The student will produce writing in a variety of forms, using the writing process, with emphasis on the essay form and technical writing.
item_specs: Items assess ability to produce well-organized essays and technical documents using the full writing process.
essential_questions: How does purpose determine the form and style of writing?
learning_targets: I can write a well-organized multi-paragraph essay.
I can produce clear technical writing appropriate to audience and purpose.
sample_assessment: Which writing style is MOST appropriate for a lab report? A) First-person narrative with opinions B) Objective third-person with precise language C) Informal with slang D) Persuasive with emotional appeals

benchmark: The student will make persuasive presentations and participate in debates, using evidence and rhetorical strategies.
item_specs: Items assess ability to construct and deliver persuasive arguments and rebuttals in formal debate settings.
essential_questions: How do effective speakers use rhetoric to persuade?
learning_targets: I can construct and present a persuasive argument with supporting evidence.
I can effectively rebut opposing arguments in a debate.
sample_assessment: In a debate, a rebuttal is — A) A new argument introduced at the end B) A direct response that challenges the opposing side's argument C) A summary of your own position D) A question asked to the audience

benchmark: The student will analyze and evaluate informational and literary nonfiction texts, including identifying rhetorical strategies and evaluating their effectiveness.
item_specs: Items require analyzing how authors use rhetorical strategies to achieve their purpose and evaluating effectiveness.
essential_questions: How do authors use rhetorical strategies to achieve their purpose?
learning_targets: I can identify and analyze rhetorical strategies in nonfiction texts.
I can evaluate the effectiveness of an author's rhetorical choices.
sample_assessment: An author uses short, choppy sentences to describe a battle scene. This syntactical choice most likely creates a feeling of — A) Calm reflection B) Urgency and tension C) Humor D) Sadness

benchmark: The student will read, comprehend, and analyze literary texts from different periods and cultures, including identifying universal themes.
item_specs: Items require comparing themes across texts from different time periods and cultures.
essential_questions: What universal themes connect literature across time and cultures?
learning_targets: I can identify universal themes in literature from different cultures.
I can analyze how historical and cultural context influences a literary work.
sample_assessment: The theme of 'the hero's journey' appears in Greek mythology, medieval literature, and modern films. This is an example of — A) A universal theme B) Plagiarism C) A coincidence D) Historical fiction

benchmark: The student will write well-developed compositions with clear, precise vocabulary and varied sentence structure for a variety of audiences and purposes.
item_specs: Items assess ability to adapt writing style for different audiences and purposes while maintaining clarity.
essential_questions: How do audience and purpose shape writing choices?
learning_targets: I can adjust my writing style for different audiences and purposes.
I can use precise vocabulary and varied sentence structure.
sample_assessment: A student is writing a letter to a state senator about a local issue. Which tone is MOST appropriate? A) Casual and humorous B) Formal and respectful C) Angry and accusatory D) Sarcastic and dismissive

benchmark: The student will read, comprehend, and analyze American literature from the colonial period through the contemporary era, relating literary works to their historical contexts.
item_specs: Items require connecting American literary works to their historical periods and analyzing how context shapes themes and style.
essential_questions: How does American literature reflect the historical experiences of the nation?
learning_targets: I can analyze how American literary works reflect their historical period.
I can trace the development of American literary movements.
sample_assessment: Which literary movement emphasized self-reliance, individualism, and a connection to nature? A) Naturalism B) Transcendentalism C) Realism D) Puritanism

benchmark: The student will analyze how authors of American literature use elements of style, including diction, syntax, and rhetorical devices.
item_specs: Items require close analysis of author's craft in American literary texts.
essential_questions: How do American authors use style to convey meaning and perspective?
learning_targets: I can analyze an author's diction and syntax choices.
I can explain how rhetorical devices enhance an author's message.
sample_assessment: Martin Luther King Jr.'s repeated use of 'I have a dream...' is an example of — A) Antithesis B) Anaphora C) Chiasmus D) Zeugma

benchmark: The student will write a research paper, using primary and secondary sources, with proper documentation using MLA or APA format.
item_specs: Items assess ability to locate, evaluate, and synthesize sources and document them using standard formats.
essential_questions: How do we conduct ethical, thorough academic research?
learning_targets: I can write a research paper using primary and secondary sources.
I can correctly document sources using MLA or APA format.
sample_assessment: Which is an example of a primary source for a research paper on the Civil War? A) A textbook chapter about the Civil War B) A letter written by a soldier during the Civil War C) A documentary about the Civil War D) An encyclopedia entry about the Civil War

benchmark: The student will analyze and evaluate informational and persuasive texts, including identifying logical fallacies and evaluating the validity of arguments.
item_specs: Items require identifying specific logical fallacies and evaluating the validity of complex arguments.
essential_questions: How do we identify flawed reasoning in arguments?
learning_targets: I can identify common logical fallacies in arguments.
I can evaluate the validity of complex arguments across multiple texts.
sample_assessment: A politician says, 'If we allow students to use phones in school, next they'll want to skip class entirely.' This is an example of — A) Ad hominem B) Red herring C) Slippery slope D) Straw man

benchmark: The student will read, comprehend, and analyze British and world literature, including major literary periods and movements.
item_specs: Items require analyzing works from Anglo-Saxon through contemporary British and world literature.
essential_questions: How have major literary periods shaped the development of literature?
learning_targets: I can analyze works from major British and world literary periods.
I can compare literary movements across different cultures and time periods.
sample_assessment: The epic poem 'Beowulf' belongs to which literary period? A) Renaissance B) Anglo-Saxon C) Victorian D) Romantic

benchmark: The student will analyze how literary forms and genres have evolved, including the development of the novel, drama, poetry, and essay.
item_specs: Items require tracing the development of literary genres and explaining how forms have changed over time.
essential_questions: How have literary genres evolved over time?
learning_targets: I can trace the development of major literary genres.
I can explain how literary forms reflect changes in society and culture.
sample_assessment: Shakespeare's plays are primarily written in — A) Free verse B) Iambic pentameter C) Haiku D) Prose

benchmark: The student will write in a variety of modes for various audiences and purposes, demonstrating command of standard English conventions and sophisticated style.
item_specs: Items assess sophisticated writing skills including voice, style, and command of conventions.
essential_questions: How do advanced writers develop a distinctive and effective voice?
learning_targets: I can write with a distinctive voice appropriate to audience and purpose.
I can demonstrate sophisticated command of grammar, usage, and mechanics.
sample_assessment: Which revision BEST improves the following sentence: 'The thing that happened was really bad and affected a lot of people'? A) The thing was very bad for many people. B) The catastrophic flood displaced thousands of residents. C) It was bad and hurt people. D) A bad thing happened to lots of people.

benchmark: The student will develop a research paper by synthesizing information from multiple sources, evaluating the quality and reliability of sources, and presenting findings with proper documentation.
item_specs: Items assess advanced research skills including synthesis across sources and evaluation of source quality.
essential_questions: How do we synthesize information from diverse sources into a cohesive argument?
learning_targets: I can synthesize information from diverse sources into a cohesive research paper.
I can evaluate the quality and reliability of academic sources.
sample_assessment: When synthesizing information from three sources that provide different perspectives on an issue, a researcher should — A) Choose the source that agrees with their opinion B) Present only the most recent source C) Integrate all perspectives, analyzing their strengths and limitations D) Ignore sources that disagree with each other

benchmark: The student will analyze and critique film, media, and digital texts, applying the same critical analysis skills used for print texts.
item_specs: Items require applying literary analysis concepts to film, digital media, and multimedia texts.
essential_questions: How do we apply critical analysis skills to non-print texts?
learning_targets: I can analyze film and media using the same critical frameworks used for literature.
I can evaluate the techniques used in digital and multimedia texts.
sample_assessment: A filmmaker uses a low camera angle to show a character. This technique most likely makes the character appear — A) Weak and vulnerable B) Powerful and dominant C) Mysterious and hidden D) Friendly and approachable
